coredata.dc:description: Wound infections caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) present a significant challenge to wound healing. This has motivated the development of novel antibiotic-free agents. In this study, ruthenium-doped carbon dots (Ru-CDs) with photodynamic antibacterial activity were synthesized to treat MRSA-infected skin wounds. The Ru-CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using Ru-Aphen as the nitrogen source and citric acid as the carbon source, resulting in uniform spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 2.7 ± 0.8 nm. Singlet oxygen generation was observed when the Ru-CDs were exposed to light. In vitro experiments showed concentration- and light-dependent antibacterial activity of the Ru-CDs against MRSA, with 99.9 % bacterial reduction when treated with 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs under light for 10 min. A significant level of intracellular ROS was observed, and microscopy confirmed bacterial membrane disruption. Biocompatibility tests showed no significant toxicity, and in vivo studies on rabbit wound models demonstrated effective antibacterial activity under light conditions and enhanced wound healing compared to controls. The results collectively highlight the potential of Ru-CDs as an antibiotic-free agent for treating antibiotic resistant bacterial infections through photodynamic generation of extracellular ROS and induction of intracellular ROS.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound infections caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) present a significant challenge to wound healing. This has motivated the development of novel antibiotic-free agents. In this study, ruthenium-doped carbon dots (Ru-CDs) with photodynamic antibacterial activity were synthesized to treat MRSA-infected skin wounds. The Ru-CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using Ru-Aphen as the nitrogen source and citric acid as the carbon source, resulting in uniform spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 2.7 ± 0.8 nm. Singlet oxygen generation was observed when the Ru-CDs were exposed to light. In vitro experiments showed concentration- and light-dependent antibacterial activity of the Ru-CDs against MRSA, with 99.9 % bacterial reduction when treated with 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs under light for 10 min. A significant level of intracellular ROS was observed, and microscopy confirmed bacterial membrane disruption. Biocompatibility tests showed no significant toxicity, and in vivo studies on rabbit wound models demonstrated effective antibacterial activity under light conditions and enhanced wound healing compared to controls. The results collectively highlight the potential of Ru-CDs as an antibiotic-free agent for treating antibiotic resistant bacterial infections through photodynamic generation of extracellular ROS and induction of intracellular ROS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are commonly used to treat a variety of bacterial infections including skin wound infections [1–4]. However, the overuse of antibiotics has led to the widespread of antibiotic resistance [4]. In the U.S. alone, antibiotic resistant bacteria account for at least 2 million infections and 23,000 deaths annually, leading to an economic loss of $55–70 billion per year [5]. Many bacteria exhibit resistance to multiple antibiotics, including the last-resort antibiotic vancomycin [6,7]. For example, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) has emerged as one of the most notorious pathogens of skin wound infections that causes nonfunctional scarring and/or prolonged healing times [8–10]. Notably, healthcare-associated environments, particularly medical packaging, also have been identified as critical reservoirs for the transmission of drug-resistant pathogens, further exacerbating the challenge of infection control. The proliferation of these multidrug-resistant bacteria has raised global concerns about returning to the pre-antibiotic era [11–13]. There is a growing interest and pressing need to develop antibiotic-free treatment strategies.

Among various potential strategies, carbon-based nanomaterials with unique nanoscale, tunable physical and chemical properties, and high biocompatibility represent promising candidates for antimicrobial treatment, including the treatment of wound infections [14–16]. Carbon-based nanomaterials exert their antimicrobial effects through several mechanisms, including physical and mechanical disruption [17,18], photothermal action [19,20], chemical catalysis [21], metabolic inhibition [22,23], the induction of oxidative stress [24,25], and synergistic interaction of multiple antibacterial mechanisms [26–28]. Among them, oxidative stress is recognized as a highly effective and non-selective antibacterial mechanisms. It is triggered by reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (OH·), and singlet oxygen (1O2) [29,30]. ROS can be generated by carbon-based nanomaterials upon exposure to external energy stimuli. For instance, electrocatalytic carbon-based nanomaterials can reduce oxygen to H2O2 via two-electron transfer, though their reliance on external power supplies and potential byproduct toxicity limits broader application [31–33]. Photocatalytic nanomaterials can also generate ROS in the presence of ultraviolet (UV) and near-infrared (NIR) light [30]. However, ultraviolet light may cause skin cancer and DNA damage [34,35], while NIR light can induce thermal effects [36,37], which may hinder the catalytic treatment process [38]. In comparison to UV and NIR light, visible light exhibits superior biocompatibility, a wider range of material adaptability, and diminished thermal effects in photodynamic therapy. Therefore, carbon-based nanomaterials capable of visible light-based photodynamic therapy hold considerable promise for antibiotic-free treatment of wound infections.

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm, have attracted growing interest in antimicrobial applications for their ability to generate external ROS [39]. In the presence of H2O2, CDs can catalyze the formation of OH·, thereby inhibiting bacterial growth [40]. Recent studies have demonstrated that CDs can also exhibit antimicrobial activity by inducing intracellular ROS generation [41]. The antibacterial efficiency of CDs was further enhanced through surface functionalization, which promoted bacterial adhesion and improved ROS generation [42,43]. Based on these findings, it is possible that extracellular and intracellular ROS could synergistically enhance antimicrobial efficiency. Such enhancement can be realized through heteroatom doping, particularly with metals such as Fe, Mn, and Cu [44–47]. For example, Fe doping facilitates Fenton or Fenton-like reactions with H2O2 to generate abundant OH·, rapidly damaging bacterial membranes and essential biomolecules [48,49]. Mn doping has also been shown to accelerate electron transfer for enhanced ROS production, while Cu doping exhibits catalase-like and peroxidase-like catalytic activity to increases oxidative stress within bacterial cells [47,50].

Among various dopants, Ruthenium (Ru) as a platinum group element offers significant advantages, particularly in photodynamic therapy, owing to its variable oxidation state, exceptional catalytic properties, antioxidant capacity, and biocompatibility [51–54]. Studies have shown that Ru(II/III) complexes exhibit activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria through multiple mechanisms, including DNA/RNA binding, membrane disruption, and light-induced ROS generation [55–57]. However, the application of Ru-doped carbon-based nanomaterials is limited by laborious synthesis methods. For example, the synthesis of Ru and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanowires (NWs) was achieved through a multi-step process involving controlled dispersion, prolonged reaction, solvent removal, and high-temperature pyrolysis under a nitrogen atmosphere [58]. Under carefully regulated thermolysis conditions achieved with a series of thermal analysis and microscopic monitoring, buckyruthenocene yielded small ruthenium nanoparticles that exhibited high catalytic hydrogenation activity [59]. A Ru-doped core/shell structured hybrid catalyst were synthesized through a multi-step route involving the dispersion of oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs), controlled metal ion addition, precipitation with ammonium bicarbonate, and lyophilization, followed by high-temperature sulfurization under a nitrogen atmosphere in a precisely regulated tube furnace [60]. Similarly, Ru-doped CDs (Ru-CDs) were synthesized through a cumbersome procedure, including dissolution of metal precursors and CDs in deionized water, hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon-lined autoclave, centrifugal separation, vacuum drying, and final annealing under an argon atmosphere at controlled temperatures [61]. To promote the application of Ru-CDs in antimicrobial applications, it is of practical significance to develop a facile method for synthesizing Ru-CDs capable of photodynamically generating extracellular ROS and inducing intracellular ROS.

The primary objective of this study was to synthesize Ru-CDs as an antibiotic-free agent to treat skin wounds infected by MRSA. The skin was selected as a model system because it act as a key barrier against harmful impacts such as microbial invasion [62–64]. This important function can be compromised when the skin is damaged [65], and damaged skin becomes susceptible to bacterial infections, causing burdens to the health system [66,67]. To achieve the objective, Ru-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Compared with the pyrolysis method used in previous studies [68–70], the hydrothermal approach can be carried out under mild reaction conditions, achieve precise morphology control, and enhance the water solubility of Ru-CDs [71–74]. The latter is particularly desirable as high solubility can improve the bioavailability and effectiveness of Ru-CDs in antimicrobial applications by ensuring better dispersion and interactions with microbial cells [75,76]. The antibacterial activity of the synthesized Ru-CDs was evaluated with in vitro treatment of MRSA. Subsequently, biocompatibility assessments and in vivo experiments were conducted using an MRSA-infected rabbit skin defect model to further examine the antibacterial activity of the Ru-CDs. To elucidate the antibacterial mechanisms, the production of extracellular and intracellular ROS was detected with assays and probes, and bacterial membrane damage was evaluated with microscopy and live/dead staining assays. The findings of this study can offer valuable insight into the development and application of biocompatible carbon-based nanomaterials in both biomedical and environmental fields.

Ru-CDs were synthesized by modifying a previously reported one-step hydrothermal method [77]. Briefly, 10 mg of Ru-Aphen was mixed in 20 mL of 0.1 g/mL citric acid solution under ultrasonication for 20 min. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred to a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 185 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was neutralized with saturated sodium carbonate. The supernatant was collected with centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 5 min and subsequently dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off 500–1000 Da, Spectrum Co., Ltd).

The morphology and structure of the synthesized Ru-CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (Talos F200X, Thermo Scientific). The chemical element composition of the Ru-CDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific). A quantitative analysis of the ruthenium doped in the Ru-CDs was performed using Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS) (7900 ICP-MS, Agilent). The chemical structure and functional groups of the Ru-CDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific) and UV–visible spectroscopy (Cary 100, Agilent). The fluorescence properties of the Ru-CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi).

The ROS generated by the Ru-CDs were detected using 9,10-Anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA), a singlet oxygen probe. Briefly, a 10 mM stock solution of ABDA was prepared by dissolving 5 mg of ABDA in 1.218 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide, which was then diluted to a final concentration of 0.2 mM. Subsequently, 500 μL of the diluted ABDA solution (0.2 mM) was combined with 500 μL of the Ru-CDs solution (0.29 mg/mL) to obtain a final mixture with 0.1 mM ABDA and 0.145 mg/mL Ru-CDs. Two final mixtures were prepared, and the 0.1 mM ABDA solution was designated as a control. One mixture sample and the ABDA control were exposed to white light (12 W, 20 cm distance), and their UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded at 0 s, 10 s, 20 s, 40 s, 80 s, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, and 30 min. Meanwhile, the second mixture sample was maintained under identical conditions without light and its UV–Vis spectra was obtained. The spectra collected under these different conditions were then compared for further evaluation. To understand the reaction kinetics, relative absorbance was fitted as pseudo-first order (Eq. (1)) and double exponential (Eq. (2)) equations:

The methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) strain (ATCC 43300) was cultured in Tryptic Soy Broth (TSB), while Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus, ATCC 25923) and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus epidermidis (MRSE, ATCC 51625) were cultured in LB medium. Single colonies were isolated using the streak plate method. Subsequently, colonies were selected and incubated in 5 mL TSB medium at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm for 8 h to achieve the logarithmic growth phase. The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized Ru-CDs was investigated using the plate coating method. To assess the dose-dependent and illumination time-dependent effects, bacterial cells diluted to a final concentration of 1 × 106 colony-forming units (CFU)/mL were initially incubated with varying concentrations of Ru-CDs (0 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, and 100 μg/mL) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at 37 °C for 1 h, with shaking at 200 rpm. Subsequently, the bacteria-CD mixture was exposed to white light at a power of 12 watts for 0, 5, 8, and 10 min, with the light source positioned at a distance of 20 cm. After illumination, 50 μL of the bacteria-CD mixture was spread onto a TSB agar plate and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. Colony counts were performed during the incubation period. Following the above-described procedure for evaluating dose- and illumination time-dependent effects, a live/dead assay was performed using Bacterial Viability and Counting Kit (#L34856, Invitrogen™). The bacterial concentration was adjusted to 108 CFU/mL, and four treatment groups were prepared: (1) control (no Ru-CDs, no light), (2) light-only (no Ru-CDs, 10-min light exposure), (3) dark (100 μg/mL Ru-CDs, no light), and (4) experimental (100 μg/mL Ru-CDs, 10-min light exposure). After treatment, the samples were centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was discarded, and the dye was added and incubated at room temperature for 15 min. The stained cells were observed using a fluorescence microscope (DMi8, Leica Microsystems).

To investigate the role of ROS in the photodynamic antibacterial activity of Ru-CDs, we performed additional experiments using N-acetylcysteine (NAC), a potent ROS scavenger. Bacterial suspensions of MRSA and S. aureus were treated with Ru-CDs (50 μg/mL) in the presence or absence of NAC, followed by light irradiation (5 min). Bacterial viability was assessed using CFU assays.

To further characterize the morphology of the bacteria, the bacteria-CD mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and resuspended in PBS to maintain a Ru-CDs concentration of 100 μg/mL and a cell concentration of 109 CFU/mL. This process was repeated twice. The samples were then fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at 4 °C for 12 h, followed by dehydration through a graded series of ethanol concentrations (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 %). Finally, the bacterial morphology was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Phenom ProX, Thermo Scientific). According to the instructions of the ROS detection kit (beyotime, China), DCFH-DA was pre-incubated with the MRSA. Subsequently, the dose- and illumination time-dependent antibacterial experiment was conducted, and intracellular ROS production was observed using a fluorescence microscope.

The biocompatibility of the Ru-CDs was assessed using the CCK-8 assay (kit catalog #CK042, DOJINDO Co., Ltd) following the manufacturer's standard protocol. Briefly, the L929 and RAW 264.7 macrophage cells were cultured in DMEM/F12 or DMEM, respectively, at 37 °C under 10 % CO₂ conditions. The culture medium was supplemented with 10 % (v/v) qualified fetal bovine serum and 50 μg/mL gentamicin. Cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. L929 cells were incubated with different concentrations of Ru-CDs (10 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL), followed by light exposure for different durations (0 min, 15 min, 30 min). The cells were then co-cultured for 24 h, after which cell viability was measured. In addition, live/dead experiments were performed according to the manufacturer's instructions using the Cell Double Staining (calcein-AM/PI) kit (catalog #CS01, DOJINDO Co., Ltd), to further confirm the biocompatibility of Ru-CDs. The hemolysis assay was conducted to evaluate the hemolytic effect of Ru-CDs at different concentrations (20 μg, 50 μg, 100 μg, 200 μg, and 400 μg). Fresh red blood cells were collected, washed with PBS, and resuspended to the desired concentration. The red blood cell suspension was then incubated with Ru-CDs at 37 °C for 2 h. After incubation, the mixtures were centrifuged, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 540 nm using a spectrophotometer to determine the release of hemoglobin. Hemolysis percentage was calculated using deionized water as the positive control (100 % hemolysis) and PBS as the negative control (0 % hemolysis).

In vivo experiments were conducted using 2 to 3 months-old male New Zealand white rabbits (Yuhang Kelian Rabbit Industry Co., Ltd). After anesthesia and hair removal, a round skin wound with approximately 2.5 cm in diameter was established on each rabbit. One hundred microliters of the MRSA suspension (1 × 106 CFU/mL) were dropped into the wound to construct a skin defect infection model. After 12 h of infection, the rabbits were divided into four groups: (1) the infected group under dark, (2) the infected group under light, (3) the infected group treated with 100 μL of 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs under dark and (4) the infected group treated with 100 μL of 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs under light. The wound healing conditions were recorded with photographs and the body weights were measured on days 1, 3, 5, and 7. After 7 days, the rabbits were euthanized under anesthesia, and the dorsal wound tissues and major organs were excised, fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde overnight, dehydrated, and embedded in paraffin for subsequent histopathological staining. The paraffin-embedded wax blocks were sectioned using a microtome, followed by dewaxing and serial sectioning. The sections were then stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome stain. All experimental procedures were conducted at the Experimental Animal Center of Wenzhou Medical University under license SYXK (Zhejiang) 2019–0009, in strict accordance with the Chinese Ethical Guidelines (GB/T 35892–2018) and were approved by the Experimental Animal Ethics Committee (xmsq2024–0701).

All experiments were conducted with a minimum of three replicates, and the results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was determined using an unpaired two-sided Student's t-test. Significance levels are denoted as follows: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and NS indicates no significance.

The Ru-CD solution obtained with Ru-Aphen and citric acid as the precursors via a hydrothermal method was clear and showed a bright yellow color. Compared to AIE-based photosensitizers that require multi-step synthesis and metal-catalyzed reactions, Ru-CDs can be easily prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method with no need for complex organic reactions or expensive catalysts [78]. This synthetic simplicity highlights the practical potential of Ru-CDs. After collection and re-suspension in pure water and PBS, the Ru-CDs remained dispersed without aggregation for 30 days (Supporting Information (SI) Fig. S1), demonstrating their superior water solubility and stability. As shown in the HRTEM image in Fig. 1A, the synthesized Ru-CDs exhibited a relatively uniform size distribution. The particle diameters of Ru-CDs ranged from 1 to 5 nm, with an average of 2.7 ± 0.8 nm (SI Fig. S2). Additionally, the HRTEM image clearly revealed lattice spacing in the Ru-CDs (Fig. 1B), indicating the possible presence of Ru-related active sites that may enhance light absorption by modifying the electronic structure [79–81]. To further investigate the structural characteristics of the Ru-CDs, XRD analysis was performed. As shown in SI Fig. S3, A broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ ≈ 20°-30° was observed, indicating the amorphous or low-crystalline nature of the Ru-CDs [82]. This feature is typically attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic-like carbon structures with disordered interlayer stacking [83,84].

FTIR spectra (Fig. 1C) revealed multiple characteristic peaks for the Ru-CDs. The peaks observed at 3435, 1582, 1385, and 1184 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of -OH, CC, CN, and CO, respectively. These results indicate the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the Ru-CDs, which facilitate hydrogen bond formation and interaction with polar solvents [85,86]. It can explain the outstanding water solubility of the Ru-CDs. XPS was employed to further characterize the elemental composition of the Ru-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D & SI Fig. S4, peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, Ru3p, and O1s were observed in the XPS spectrum at 284.8 eV, 402.4 eV, 470.2 eV, and 534.7 eV, respectively. The peak spectrum of C1s could be decomposed into four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, 287.6 eV and 289 eV, which could be attributed to C=C/C-C, C − O/C − N, O − C=O and CO, respectively. The O1s peak spectrum was observed with three distinct peaks at 531.1 eV, 532.2 eV, and 535.4 eV, which could be attributed to C − O − C/C − O − H, CO, and H2O, respectively. Additionally, two peaks at 462.1 eV and 484.3 eV were detected in the Ru spectrum (SI Fig. S5), corresponding to Ru 3p3/2 and Ru 3p1/2, respectively. According to the XPS quantitative analysis, the Ru-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, O, and Ru, with atomic percentages of C (67.14 %), N (4.82 %), O (27.84 %), and Ru (0.20 %). The ruthenium element concentration was measured to be 0.7 mg/L ICP-MS. These results confirmed successful synthesis of the Ru-CDs with C, N, O, and Ru are the primary elements.

UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed to analyze the optical properties of the Ru-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1E, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the Ru-CDs exhibited peaks near 265 nm and 448 nm, which might be attributed to the intraligand transition absorption and the electronic transition from Ru2+ to metal-to-ligand charge transfer, respectively [87,88]. In addition, no significant changes were observed in the absorption spectrum of the Ru-Aphen complex after transformation into Ru-CDs, indicating that the parent structure of the complex was retained following the hydrothermal reaction with no alteration in its properties. The fluorescence spectrum of Ru-CDs (Fig. 1F) showed a maximum emission peak at 613 nm and a maximum excitation peak at 448 nm. It suggested that the large Stokes shift (~165 nm) reduced self-absorption and enhanced fluorescence detection efficiency [89,90]. Meanwhile, the 613 nm red emission exhibits enhanced penetration capability, making it well-suited for biomedical and environmental applications [91,92].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro generation of ROS by Ru-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the synthesized Ru-CDs to generate ROS was analyzed using the ABDA-based ROS detection experiments under various conditions. The ABDA probe exhibited a characteristic and highly selective ultraviolet absorption peak (~376 nm), and its absorbance was high selectivity and significantly reduced when oxidized by singlet oxygen (1O2). As shown in Fig. 2A, the absorbance of the Ru-CDs and ABDA mixture remained nearly constant over 480 s under dark conditions, indicating negligible ROS generation in the absence of light. The absorbance of both the ABDA control (without Ru-CDs) and the mixed solution of Ru-CDs and ABDA decreased continuously over time under illumination (Figs. 2B & 2C). However, the reduction rate of the absorbance of the ABDA control was significantly lower compared to that of the mixed solution. This indicated that the ABDA probe in the mixed solution captured 1O2 produced by the Ru-CDs, confirming the photodynamic effect of the Ru-CDs.

The change in relative absorbance at 376 nm for each group was compared (Fig. 2D). The ABDA control and the mixed solution of Ru-CDs and ABDA under illumination exhibited approximately 24 % and 84 % decrease in relative absorbance, respectively. The kinetics of ROS generation was analyzed by using a pseudo-first order kinetic equation for the ABDA control under illumination and a double exponential model equation for the CD-ABDA mixture under illumination (SI Fig. S6) [93–95]. The ABDA control under illumination undergoes very slow (kobs = 0.00058 s−1), indicating negligible illumination involvement. In comparison the CD-ABDA mixture show a high initial reaction rate (k1 = 0.0266 s−1), suggesting that Ru-CDs as a photosensitizer significantly enhanced energy transfer and produced a large amount of ROS [96,97]. The slow decay of the rate (k2 = 0.0057 s−1) in the later stage might result from the reduced solubility and diffusion rate of oxygen in the solution. Compared with Ni-ZnO@C and AIE-based photosensitizers, Ru-CDs exhibit comparable or enhanced ROS generation efficiency under visible light, as reflected by a high singlet oxygen generation rate (k₁ = 0.0266 s−1) [78,98]. These results collectively confirmed the ability of the synthesized Ru-CDs to photodynamically generate ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antimicrobial activity of Ru-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photodynamic antimicrobial activity of the synthesized Ru-CDs was investigated by exposing methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) under a series of Ru-CD concentrations (0, 20, 50, and 100 μg/mL) and light exposure times (0, 5, 8, and 10 min). In the absence of Ru-CDs (concentration 0 μg/mL), a decrease of 33.9 % in bacterial survival rate was observed as the light exposure time increased from 0 to 10 min (Figs. 3A & 3B), which could be attributed to the antibacterial effect of blue light with a wave length of 400–600 nm [99,100]. Under dark conditions (light exposure 0 min), the bacterial survival rate decreased by 18.3 % when the Ru-CDs concentration increased from 0 to 100 μg/mL. Because Ru-CDs did not generate any ROS in the dark (Fig. 2A), the antibacterial effect of Ru-CDs observed in the absence of light might result from their ability to induce the generation of intracellular ROS. Although MRSA was inactivated under these control conditions, its survival rate was significantly lower in the presence of both Ru-CDs and light. For example, after 10 min of light exposure, the bacterial survival rates decreased sharply in a dose-dependent manner, reaching 42.5 %, 8.0 % and 0.1 % at Ru-CDs concentrations of 20, 50, and 100 μg/mL, respectively.

To further validate the clinical applicability of Ru-CDs, the antibacterial evaluation was extended to two additional staphylococcal strains: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), a common skin pathogen, and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus epidermidis (MRSE), a clinically relevant multidrug-resistant strain frequently associated with wound infections and infections related to indwelling medical devices [2,101–104]. As shown in SI Fig. S7-S10, Ru-CDs exhibited strong, light- and concentration-dependent antibacterial activity against both strains. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, bacterial viability decreased to 1 % for S. aureus and 5 % for MRSE following 10 min of light exposure. The results collectively suggest that the photodynamic antimicrobial effects of the Ru-CDs are dependent on dose and illumination time, consistent with their mechanism of light-triggered ROS generation. These findings further support the potential of Ru-CDs as a broad-spectrum photodynamic antibacterial agent for treating clinically relevant drug-resistant infections, particularly in wound healing scenarios.

To further investigate the antimicrobial properties of Ru-CDs, live-dead staining experiments were conducted on MRSA subjected to different treatments using SYTO-9 and propidium iodide (PI). SYTO-9 permeates both live and dead bacterial membranes, staining all cells green, while PI selectively penetrates compromised membranes of dead cells, staining them red. As shown in Fig. 3C, in the dark control group, MRSA exhibited an even distribution with strong green fluorescence and no detectable red fluorescence, indicating no cell death. In the illuminated control group, strong green fluorescence was observed, with only a few bacteria stained red, indicating minimal bacterial death. Similarly, the Ru-CDs control group (100 μg/mL Ru-CDs + dark) exhibited a large amount of green fluorescence, with red fluorescence present only in a small proportion, consistent with the findings from the plate colony count shown in Figs. 3A & 3B. In contrast, in the Ru-CDs group (100 μg/mL Ru-CDs + light), MRSA appeared in small aggregates, with significantly diminished or nearly absent green fluorescence but predominant red fluorescence, indicating extensive bacterial death.

SEM was employed to observe the morphological changes in bacteria before and after photodynamic treatment with the Ru-CDs (Fig. 3D). The SEM images of the MRSA from the dark control group, light control group, and dark Ru-CDs group displayed the typical morphology of MRSA, characterized by clusters forming irregular grape-like arrangements, with regular spherical structures and smooth, intact surfaces. These features are consistent with Gram-positive cocci and reflect their typical morphology under normal physiological conditions. Following the addition of 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs and 10 min of light exposure, the cells became dispersed and damaged, with the originally regular spherical bacteria displaying irregular shapes and blurred outlines, indicating significant changes in the cellular structure. The morphological changes could be due to compromised integrity of the cell membrane, a critical component of bacterial respiration and the primary target of reactive oxygen species [105,106]. These observations suggested that the photodynamic effect of Ru-CDs generated substances (e.g., intracellular ROS), which compromised the integrity of the bacterial cell membrane, ultimately leading to bacterial death. Zeta potential analysis further confirmed that Ru-CDs exhibit a negative surface charge (−20 ± 3 mV). Given that bacterial membranes, including those of MRSA, also possess a net negative charge, electrostatic repulsion reduces the likelihood of nanoparticle internalization [107,108]. Therefore, Ru-CDs are more likely to adsorb onto the bacterial membrane surface rather than enter the cytoplasm [109,110].

The antibiofilm activity of Ru-CDs was further evaluated by treating preformed MRSA biofilms with different concentrations of Ru-CDs followed by SYTO9/PI staining (SI Fig. S11). In the untreated group, dense green fluorescence indicated an intact and viable biofilm. In contrast, the Ru-CDs-treated groups showed an increase in red fluorescence and a corresponding decrease in green signal, especially at 100 μg/mL, indicating significant bacterial death and biofilm disruption. These results suggest that Ru-CDs can effectively penetrate and damage established MRSA biofilms.

To examine the ability of the Ru-CDs to induce intracellular ROS, MRSA treated with various Ru-CDs conditions was tested with the DCFH-DA ROS probe (Fig. 4 ), which detects multiple types of ROS such as H2O2 and ·OH. Notably, a significant level of intracellular ROS was observed only in the presence of both Ru-CDs and light, confirming the involvement of this mechanism in the antibacterial activity of Ru-CDs. Previous studies have demonstrated that intracellular ROS play a crucial role in the antibacterial activity by damaging vital bacterial components such as cell membranes, DNA, and proteins, and ultimately leading to bacterial inactivation [105,111]. The intracellular ROS induced by Ru-CDs might cause oxidative stress in MRSA cells when ROS levels exceed the neutralizing capacity of their antioxidant systems [112,113]. To further verify the ROS-mediated mechanism, we employed N-acetylcysteine (NAC), a commonly used ROS scavenger, to assess its impact on the antibacterial activity of Ru-CDs. The results demonstrated that NAC significantly restored bacterial viability under Ru-CDs combined with light treatment, suggesting that the photodynamic antibacterial effect of Ru-CDs is largely dependent on ROS accumulation (SI Fig. S12 & S13). These findings, together with those from plate counting, live/dead images, and SEM images, collectively demonstrate that the Ru-CDs are capable of inactivating antibiotic resistant bacteria potentially through photodynamic generation of extracellular ROS and induction of intracellular ROS.

Prior to in vivo application of the synthesized Ru-CDs, it is essential to confirm their biocompatibility and non-toxicity to the human body. The biocompatibility of the Ru-CDs was initially assessed using a CCK-8 cell viability assay on mice fibroblast (L929) cells (Fig. 5A). After 24 h of incubation with 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs under dark conditions, cell viability was not significantly different from that of the dark and light control groups. Similarly, with 100 μg/mL Ru-CDs and 30 mins of illumination, L929 cell viability remained high at 95 % and had no significant difference from the control groups. Additionally, the cytocompatibility of Ru-CDs toward immune cells was evaluated using RAW 264.7 macrophages. As shown in SI Fig. S14, Ru-CDs maintained good biocompatibility even under high concentrations and light condition, further supporting their potential for biomedical applications. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of Ru-CDs was compared with other reported photodynamic materials. For example, 3 % Ni-ZnO@C nanoparticles reduced cell viability below 80 % at concentrations above 100 μg/mL after 24 h, whereas Ru-CDs maintained about 95 % viability at 100 μg/mL under the same conditions (Fig. 5A), demonstrating superior safety for biomedical use [98]. Additionally, the biocompatibility of the Ru-CDs was also evaluated using a live/dead cell double staining method with calcein-AM/PI. As depicted in Fig. 5B, green fluorescence was observed under all conditions, indicating negligible cell death in the presence of Ru-CDs and light. The hemolysis test was conducted to further assess the biocompatibility of the Ru-CDs (Fig. 5C). No hemolysis occurred even at a concentration of 400 μg/mL, as the hemolysis rate remained below the standard threshold of 5 % [114]. Taken together, these results demonstrate the excellent biocompatibility of the Ru-CDs and their potential applicability in photodynamic therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antimicrobial activity of Ru-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photodynamic antimicrobial activity of the Ru-CDs was further evaluated using a rabbit model with full-thickness skin defects. It was seen in Fig. 6A that the wound treated under other conditions healed noticeably slower than that with the Ru-CDs (light). On Day 3, the wound healing process of other groups did not appear to start, while the wound of the Ru-CDs (light) group had noticeably shrank. Compared to the wound area of the control group (Fig. 6B), those of the Ru-CDs (dark) group and the MRSA (light) group decreased slightly, but the difference was not statistically significant. In contrast, the wound area in the Ru-CDs (light) group decreased by approximately 50 %. By Day 7, the wound remained open in other groups but was nearly fully healed in the Ru-CDs (light) group. Compared to the control, the wound areas of the Ru-CDs (dark), MRSA (light), and Ru-CDs (light) groups were significantly reduced by 42 %, 43 %, and 10 %, respectively. In line with in vitro experiments, both light and Ru-CDs treatment under darkness exhibited minor in vivo antibacterial activity. On the other hand, the Ru-CDs under light were highly effective in treating skin wounds infected by antibiotic resistant bacteria, thereby promoting wound healing. Additionally, no significant changes in body weight were observed across all rabbit groups throughout the treatment period (Fig. 6C), indicating no negative effect of Ru-CDs on the health system with the seven-day treatment.

H&E staining was employed to assess the morphological structure of the wound section tissues (Fig. 6D). The skin wound of the Ru-CDs (light) group was significantly reduced with pronounced epidermal regeneration, improved epidermal repair, and a more complete epidermal structure. Squamous epithelial hyperplasia and coverage were observed in some areas of the wound of the Ru-CDs (light) group but not in the other groups. In the epidermis, the Ru-CDs (light) group showed reduced blood scab tissue covering the surface of the epidermal skin wound, with exudate containing numerous red blood cells, neutrophils, lymphocytes, and plasma cells. In contrast, a large amount of blood scab tissue remained in the epidermis of other groups. In the dermis, the inflammatory cell infiltration primarily consisted of neutrophils, lymphocytes, and plasma cells. Microscopic inspection suggested a noticeably lower number of infiltrating inflammatory cells per high-power field (40 magnification) in the dermis of the Ru-CDs (light) group than in other groups. The Ru-CDs (light) group exhibited abundant granulation tissue in the dermis, with the amount of granulation tissue clearly greater than that observed in other groups. Additionally, the number of fibroblasts and the extent of fibroblast proliferation in the dermis were also markedly higher compared to the other groups. Masson staining results (Fig. 6D) demonstrated that the collagen fiber density and collagen deposition in the dermis of the skin wound of the Ru-CDs (light) group were high, with observably greater collagen fiber proliferation compared to the other groups. As collagen deposition at the wound site is a critical indicator of skin defect remodeling, the results indicate a more advanced stage of wound healing after treatment with Ru-CDs and light.

To evaluate the in vivo toxicity of the Ru-CDs, tissue sections of the heart, liver, spleen, kidneys, and lungs were collected from the rabbits, followed by H&E staining. Histological analysis of the sections revealed no significant inflammatory damage or morphological disruption in the major organs of any group (Fig. 7 ). In addition, the in vivo toxicity assessment included a comprehensive analysis of blood samples from the rabbits to evaluate changes in key biochemical indicators, aiming to identify potential effects of the treatment on the hematological system (SI Table S1). In the control groups without Ru-CD treatment, especially under dark conditions, the white blood count and granulocyte absolute count were significantly elevated, indicating a pronounced inflammatory response. In contrast, the Ru-CD-treated group under light exhibited blood parameters closer to normal levels. The white blood count and granulocyte absolute were notably reduced, suggesting more effective infection control and diminished inflammation. Furthermore, the substantial increase in mean platelet volume and platelet count in the Ru-CD-treated group under light might reflect the activation of coagulation and tissue repair pathways during the infection-clearing process [115,116].

In this study, ruthenium-doped carbon dots (Ru-CDs) with photodynamic antibacterial properties were developed. The Ru-CDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method and exhibited properties favorable for antibacterial activity, including excellent solubility, stability, particle diameters ranging from 1 to 5 nm, and successful doping of ruthenium. Their therapeutic efficacy was demonstrated using in vitro experiments and a full-thickness skin defect model at different concentrations and illumination times. The Ru-CDs effectively inactivated methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus by generating extracellular ROS and inducing intracellular ROS in bacterial cells, thereby enhancing the wound healing process. The Ru-CDs also exhibited good biocompatibility. These findings demonstrated the potential of the Ru-CDs as an antibacterial agent for photodynamic treatment of skin wounds infected by antibiotic resistant bacteria.
